questions.question: Which of the following accounts is specifically prepared at the time of dissolution of a partnership firm to close all assets and liabilities?
options: Revaluation Account
Realisation Account
Profit and Loss Appropriation Account
Memorandum Revaluation Account
questions.explanation: At dissolution, a Realisation Account is prepared to close all assets and liabilities. Unlike Revaluation Account (used at admission/retirement to record change in values while the firm continues), Realisation Account is used only at dissolution. It records assets at book value on the debit side, liabilities on the credit side, and the balancing figure represents profit or loss on realisation, distributed to partners in profit-sharing ratio.

questions.question: Which of the following items is NOT transferred to the Realisation Account at the time of dissolution of a firm?
options: Sundry Debtors
Stock in Trade
Cash and Bank Balances
Bills Receivable
questions.explanation: Cash and Bank Balances are NOT transferred to Realisation Account because they are already in liquid form and serve as the medium of payment during dissolution. All other assets (Sundry Debtors, Stock, Bills Receivable, Machinery, Furniture, etc.) are transferred to the debit side of Realisation Account at their book values. Cash/Bank remains in the Cash Account and is used directly to pay liabilities and partners.

questions.question: Which of the following events leads to dissolution of PARTNERSHIP but NOT to dissolution of the FIRM?
options: Insolvency of all the partners
Court order under Section 44 of the Partnership Act
Admission of a new partner into the existing firm
Business of the firm becoming unlawful
questions.explanation: Admission of a new partner dissolves the existing partnership agreement (old partnership ends as the constitution changes) but the FIRM continues as a reconstituted partnership. The business does not close. In contrast, insolvency of all partners, court orders, and unlawful business all lead to dissolution of the FIRM itself — a complete winding up of business. The key distinction is: dissolution of firm means closure of business; dissolution of partnership means change in constitution.

questions.question: According to Section 48 of the Indian Partnership Act, 1932, the correct sequence of payment at the time of dissolution is:
options: Outside creditors → Partners' Loans → Partners' Capital
Partners' Capital → Partners' Loans → Outside Creditors
Partners' Loans → Outside Creditors → Partners' Capital
Outside Creditors → Partners' Capital → Partners' Loans
questions.explanation: Section 48 of the Indian Partnership Act prescribes a mandatory sequence: (1) Outside creditors and liabilities are paid first to protect third-party interests, (2) Partners' loans/advances to the firm are repaid next (these are distinct from capital), and (3) Partners' capital accounts are settled last. This sequence ensures that external stakeholders are fully protected before partners receive their share. Partners, as owners, bear the residual risk.

questions.question: Profit or Loss arising on realisation of assets and settlement of liabilities during dissolution is shared among partners in:
options: Capital ratio at the time of dissolution
Equal ratio always
Profit-sharing ratio
Time ratio based on years of service
questions.explanation: Realisation profit or loss is treated as a business profit or loss and is therefore distributed among all partners in their agreed profit-sharing ratio (PSR). The logic is that just as business profits and losses are shared in PSR during normal operations, the outcome of the final disposal of assets and liabilities is also a business event governed by the same ratio. Capital ratio is relevant only for interest on capital and under the Garner v Murray rule for capital deficiency caused by insolvency.

questions.question: Which of the following accounts is NOT closed through the Realisation Account at the time of dissolution?
options: Machinery Account
Creditors Account
Partners' Capital Account
Bills Receivable Account
questions.explanation: Partners' Capital Accounts are NOT closed through the Realisation Account. After completing all realisation entries and distributing profit or loss, the Capital Accounts are closed by a direct payment of cash to partners (if credit balance) or recovery from partners (if debit balance). Machinery (asset), Creditors (liability), and Bills Receivable (asset) all pass through Realisation Account. Capital accounts represent owners' equity and are settled in the final Cash Account stage.

questions.question: At the time of dissolution, fictitious assets such as Preliminary Expenses or Advertisement Suspense Account are:
options: Transferred to the debit side of Realisation Account at book value
Written off against Partners' Capital Accounts in profit-sharing ratio
Shown on the credit side of Realisation Account
Ignored as they have no monetary value
questions.explanation: Fictitious assets (Preliminary Expenses, Deferred Revenue Expenditure, Advertisement Suspense, etc.) have no realizable value — they cannot be sold. Therefore, they are NOT transferred to Realisation Account. Instead, they are written off directly against Partners' Capital Accounts in profit-sharing ratio: Dr. Partners' Capital A/cs (in PSR), Cr. Fictitious Asset A/c. This is identical to their treatment during normal operations when written off. Only tangible, realizable assets go to Realisation Account.

questions.question: Accumulated losses (Profit and Loss Account — debit balance) appearing in the Balance Sheet at the time of dissolution are:
options: Transferred to the credit side of Realisation Account
Transferred to the debit side of Realisation Account
Debited to Partners' Capital Accounts in profit-sharing ratio
Paid off by the partners equally before dissolution entries begin
questions.explanation: Accumulated losses (debit balance in P&L A/c) represent past losses already incurred by the firm. They are NOT transferred to Realisation Account (which deals with asset realisation and liability settlement). Instead, they are written off against partners' capital accounts in profit-sharing ratio: Dr. Partners' Capital A/cs (in PSR), Cr. Profit and Loss A/c. This step is completed before preparing the Realisation Account, ensuring capital accounts reflect the correct net position of each partner.

questions.question: General Reserve appearing in the Balance Sheet at dissolution is treated as:
options: Transferred to Realisation Account on the credit side
Credited to Partners' Capital Accounts in profit-sharing ratio
Used first to pay outside creditors
Transferred as a debit to Realisation Account
questions.explanation: General Reserve is an accumulated profit belonging to partners. At dissolution, it is distributed to partners in their profit-sharing ratio before Realisation Account is prepared. Entry: Dr. General Reserve A/c, Cr. Partners' Capital A/cs (in PSR). It is not transferred to Realisation Account (which only handles assets and liabilities). By crediting partners' capitals, each partner's claim from the firm increases proportionally. This is the same treatment as at any other stage of reconstitution.

questions.question: If Goodwill does NOT appear in the firm's books at dissolution, but the firm receives ₹60,000 for goodwill from the buyer, this amount is:
options: Distributed directly to Partners' Capital Accounts in PSR
Credited to Realisation Account as a receipt from asset disposal
Ignored as goodwill has no book value
Treated as capital contribution by the new buyer
questions.explanation: Regardless of whether Goodwill appears in the books, if the firm receives cash for goodwill during dissolution, the entry is: Dr. Cash/Bank A/c ₹60,000, Cr. Realisation A/c ₹60,000. The cash received becomes part of Realisation Account's credit side, contributing to the overall profit or loss on realisation. This profit is then distributed in PSR. It cannot be ignored just because there is no book entry — the cash receipt must be recorded, and Realisation Account captures all proceeds from disposal of business assets.

questions.question: At dissolution: Assets (excluding cash) at book value ₹2,50,000; realized for ₹2,30,000. Liabilities at book value ₹1,00,000; settled for ₹90,000. Realisation expenses ₹5,000. What is the Loss on Realisation?
questions.explanation: DR of Realisation A/c = Assets at BV (2,50,000) + Liabilities paid (90,000) + Expenses (5,000) = ₹3,45,000. CR of Realisation A/c = Liabilities at BV (1,00,000) + Cash realized (2,30,000) = ₹3,30,000. Loss = 3,45,000 − 3,30,000 = ₹15,000. Alternatively: Loss on assets (20,000) − Gain on liabilities (10,000) + Expenses (5,000) = ₹15,000 net loss. This loss is shared among partners in profit-sharing ratio.

questions.question: If a partner pays Realisation expenses of ₹4,000 from personal funds (firm does not reimburse), the journal entry is:
options: Dr. Realisation A/c ₹4,000; Cr. Cash/Bank A/c ₹4,000
Dr. Realisation A/c ₹4,000; Cr. Partner's Capital A/c ₹4,000
Dr. Partner's Capital A/c ₹4,000; Cr. Realisation A/c ₹4,000
Dr. Partner's Capital A/c ₹4,000; Cr. Cash/Bank A/c ₹4,000
questions.explanation: When a partner pays Realisation expenses from personal funds, the firm's cash is not involved. The correct entry is: Dr. Realisation A/c ₹4,000, Cr. Partner's Capital A/c ₹4,000. Realisation A/c is debited (the expense reduces the profit or increases the loss on realisation). Partner's Capital A/c is credited because the partner has made a payment on behalf of the firm, effectively increasing what the firm owes him. Compare this with firm paying: Dr. Realisation A/c, Cr. Cash A/c — the credit changes based on who pays.

questions.question: When a partner takes over a firm's asset at an agreed price during dissolution, which account is debited?
options: Realisation Account
Cash/Bank Account
Partner's Capital Account
The specific Asset Account
questions.explanation: When a partner takes over an asset at agreed price, the entry is: Dr. Partner's Capital A/c (agreed price), Cr. Realisation A/c (agreed price). The partner's capital is debited because he is receiving an asset in lieu of cash — his claim from the firm is reduced by the asset's agreed value. Realisation Account is credited as if the asset was 'sold' at that price, contributing to realisation proceeds. No cash changes hands; the adjustment is directly in the capital account.

questions.question: After liabilities have been transferred to Realisation Account, the entry for actual cash payment to an outside creditor is:
options: Dr. Creditor's A/c; Cr. Realisation A/c
Dr. Realisation A/c; Cr. Cash/Bank A/c
Dr. Cash/Bank A/c; Cr. Realisation A/c
Dr. Realisation A/c; Cr. Creditor's A/c
questions.explanation: Liabilities are transferred in Step 2: Dr. Creditors A/c, Cr. Realisation A/c (closing the creditor account). When actual payment is made in Step 4: Dr. Realisation A/c, Cr. Cash/Bank A/c. This records the cash outflow against Realisation Account. The difference between the liability's book value (already in CR of Realisation) and the actual amount paid (now in DR of Realisation) represents gain or loss on settlement of that liability. Individual creditor accounts are already closed in Step 2 and do not appear again.

questions.question: The Garner v Murray rule is applicable when:
options: All partners share losses in equal proportion
A partner is insolvent and cannot pay his share of capital deficiency
Realisation loss exceeds total partners' capital
A new partner is admitted without paying premium for goodwill
questions.explanation: Garner v Murray (1904) is a legal rule applicable specifically when a partner becomes insolvent at dissolution and cannot contribute his share of the capital deficiency. The rule states that solvent partners must bear the insolvent partner's capital deficiency in the ratio of their last agreed capitals (NOT profit-sharing ratio). This rule applies only to deficiency arising from insolvency — general realisation losses are always shared in PSR. It addresses the specific hardship of solvent partners when one partner's insolvency creates an irrecoverable shortfall.

questions.question: At dissolution, a partner is considered insolvent when:
options: His drawings during the year exceeded his capital contribution
He refuses to participate in dissolution proceedings
His share of realisation losses exceeds his capital balance, creating a debit balance he cannot clear from personal assets
He has retired from the firm before dissolution
questions.explanation: A partner becomes insolvent at dissolution when, after all adjustments (accumulated losses, realisation losses), his capital account shows a debit balance — meaning he owes money to the firm — and he is unable to pay this amount from his personal assets. This situation arises when total losses allocated to the partner exceed his total capital (including reserves and credits). A debit balance in the capital account = deficiency = partner's liability to the firm, which cannot be recovered if the partner is personally insolvent.

questions.question: The correct sequence for closing the books of accounts at the time of dissolution is:
options: Prepare Realisation A/c → Settle Partners' Capital A/cs → Close Cash A/c
Close Cash A/c → Prepare Realisation A/c → Settle Capital A/cs
Settle Capital A/cs → Prepare Realisation A/c → Close Cash A/c
Prepare Realisation A/c → Close Cash A/c → Settle Capital A/cs
questions.explanation: The correct sequence at dissolution: (1) Prepare Realisation Account — transfer all assets and liabilities, record proceeds and payments, find profit/loss; (2) Distribute realisation profit/loss and adjust Partners' Capital Accounts — debit/credit reserves, drawings, amounts taken over, etc.; (3) Use Cash Account to make final payments to partners or collect from them, thereby closing all Capital Accounts and Cash Account simultaneously. This sequence ensures all business transactions are settled before partners' personal dues are calculated.

questions.question: Dissolution of a firm means:
options: Change in the profit-sharing ratio among existing partners
Complete winding up of the partnership firm — all assets realized, all liabilities paid, and remaining cash distributed to partners
Retirement of one partner while business continues with remaining partners
Temporary suspension of business operations
questions.explanation: Dissolution of a firm (as distinct from dissolution of partnership) means the complete termination of the business — all assets are converted into cash, all outside liabilities and partners' loans are settled, and the remaining cash is distributed among partners to close their capital accounts. The firm ceases to exist entirely. It is different from dissolution of partnership (change in constitution) where only the partnership agreement changes but the business continues. Dissolution of firm implies dissolution of partnership, but not vice versa.

questions.question: The journal entry for transferring outside liabilities (Creditors, Bills Payable) to Realisation Account is:
options: Dr. Realisation A/c; Cr. All Liability A/cs
Dr. All Liability A/cs; Cr. Realisation A/c
Dr. Partners' Capital A/cs; Cr. Realisation A/c
Dr. Realisation A/c; Cr. Partners' Capital A/cs
questions.explanation: Outside liabilities are transferred to Realisation Account by debiting each liability account (closing it) and crediting Realisation Account: Dr. Creditors A/c, Bills Payable A/c, Outstanding Expenses A/c, etc.; Cr. Realisation A/c. The credit to Realisation Account represents the firm's obligation — an amount the firm is relieved of when liabilities are eventually settled. When actual payment is made, Dr. Realisation A/c, Cr. Cash A/c. The difference between book value and actual payment appears in Realisation Account as gain or loss on settlement.

questions.question: The Realisation Account is classified under which category of accounts?
options: Real Account — as it deals with assets
Personal Account — as it involves persons and creditors
Nominal Account — as it records gains and losses
Valuation Account — as it adjusts asset values
questions.explanation: The Realisation Account is a Nominal Account because its purpose is to record gains (profit on asset realization, discount from creditors) and losses (shortfall in asset realization, excess payments to creditors, expenses). The closing balance represents profit or loss, which is transferred to Partners' Capital Accounts. Like all Nominal Accounts, it is opened and fully closed within the dissolution period. It does not carry forward any balance — unlike Real or Personal accounts.

questions.question: Partners P and Q share profits in the ratio 3:2. At dissolution, Realisation Account shows a profit of ₹20,000. P's capital balance before adjustment = ₹50,000 (Cr). After crediting P's share of Realisation profit, P's capital balance becomes:
questions.explanation: Realisation profit of ₹20,000 is shared in the 3:2 ratio. P’s share = ₹20,000 x 3/5 = ₹12,000. P’s capital after crediting this profit = ₹50,000 + ₹12,000 = ₹62,000 credit.

questions.question: Deferred Revenue Expenditure (Advertisement Suspense Account — ₹12,000) appearing in the Balance Sheet at dissolution is:
options: Transferred to debit of Realisation Account at book value
Written off against Partners' Capital Accounts in profit-sharing ratio
Transferred to credit of Realisation Account
Recovered from the party who benefited from the advertisement
questions.explanation: Deferred Revenue Expenditure has no realizable value — it cannot be sold to a third party. Therefore, it is NOT transferred to Realisation Account. Entry: Dr. Partners' Capital A/cs (in PSR), Cr. Advertisement Suspense A/c ₹12,000. Each partner bears their share of this fictitious asset: if PSR is 2:1, Partner A bears ₹8,000 and Partner B bears ₹4,000. This treatment is identical to writing off accumulated losses. The partner whose capital is already debited shares this burden along with any realisation loss.

questions.question: At dissolution, accumulated profits such as Profit and Loss Account credit balance and General Reserve are treated by:
options: Crediting them to Realisation Account to inflate the profit
Carrying them forward as they belong to the firm
Crediting Partners' Capital Accounts in profit-sharing ratio before Realisation entries
Using them first to settle outside creditors
questions.explanation: Accumulated profits (P&L Cr. balance, General Reserve, Investment Fluctuation Reserve, etc.) belong to partners and must be distributed before Realisation entries are recorded. Entries: Dr. General Reserve A/c, Cr. Partners' Capital A/cs (in PSR); Dr. P&L A/c (Cr. balance), Cr. Partners' Capital A/cs. This step ensures each partner's capital account reflects their complete entitlement — including their share of reserves — before the final profit/loss on realisation is calculated. Reservesare NOT transferred to Realisation Account.

questions.question: Bank Overdraft appearing as a liability in the Balance Sheet at dissolution is:
options: Transferred to the debit side of Realisation Account
Deducted from cash balance before dissolution begins
Transferred to the credit side of Realisation Account like other outside liabilities
Converted to a partner's loan automatically
questions.explanation: Bank Overdraft is an outside liability (the firm owes the bank). It is treated exactly like Creditors at dissolution — transferred to the Credit side of Realisation Account: Dr. Bank Overdraft A/c, Cr. Realisation A/c. When paid off: Dr. Realisation A/c, Cr. Cash A/c. It is NOT deducted from any positive cash/bank balance directly in the books. Any positive cash balance and bank overdraft are kept in separate accounts. The bank overdraft must be settled through the dissolution process like any external obligation.

questions.question: At dissolution: Assets (excluding cash) book value ₹1,50,000, realized for ₹1,30,000. Liabilities book value ₹50,000, settled for ₹48,000. Realisation expenses ₹3,000. The net Loss on Realisation is:
questions.explanation: Method 1 — Realisation Account: DR = Assets BV (1,50,000) + Liabilities paid (48,000) + Expenses (3,000) = ₹2,01,000. CR = Liabilities BV (50,000) + Cash realized (1,30,000) = ₹1,80,000. Loss = 2,01,000 − 1,80,000 = ₹21,000. Method 2 — Component analysis: Loss on assets (20,000) − Gain on liabilities (2,000) + Expenses (3,000) = ₹21,000. The gain on liabilities (₹50,000 BV − ₹48,000 paid = ₹2,000) partially offsets the loss on assets and expenses.

questions.question: A partner's loan of ₹40,000 given to the firm at dissolution is:
options: Treated as part of capital and settled last
Transferred to the credit of Realisation Account like other liabilities
Paid after outside creditors but before capital is returned
Written off automatically against the partner's capital
questions.explanation: A partner's loan to the firm is a separate liability — distinct from capital. Per Section 48 of the Partnership Act, the order is: (1) Outside creditors first, (2) Partners' loans, (3) Partners' capital last. Entry for repayment: Dr. Partner's Loan A/c ₹40,000, Cr. Cash/Bank A/c ₹40,000. It is NOT transferred to Realisation Account (unlike outside liabilities). It is NOT merged with capital. This distinction protects partners who have lent money — they have a prior claim over capital contributors when it comes to being repaid.

questions.question: Bills Payable appearing in the Balance Sheet at dissolution is:
options: Discharged only after the bill's due date passes
Transferred to the Credit side of Realisation Account like other outside liabilities
Set off directly against Bills Receivable held by the firm
Transferred to the Debit side of Realisation Account
questions.explanation: Bills Payable is an outside liability (the firm has accepted bills in favor of creditors/suppliers). At dissolution, it is transferred to the Credit side of Realisation Account: Dr. Bills Payable A/c, Cr. Realisation A/c. When paid: Dr. Realisation A/c, Cr. Cash A/c. Bills Payable and Bills Receivable cannot be set off against each other as they involve different parties. Bills Payable is treated exactly like Creditors throughout the dissolution process — liability transferred to CR of Realisation, then payment debited to Realisation.

questions.question: Partners A and B (equal share). After all outside liabilities are settled, cash available = ₹1,10,000. Partners' Capital after adjusting Realisation profit: A = ₹70,000 (Cr), B = ₹40,000 (Cr). How much cash does Partner A receive?
questions.explanation: Each partner receives cash equal to the credit balance in their Capital Account after all adjustments. A's adjusted capital = ₹70,000 (Cr) → A receives ₹70,000. B's adjusted capital = ₹40,000 (Cr) → B receives ₹40,000. Total = ₹1,10,000 = Cash available ✓. Entry: Dr. A's Capital A/c ₹70,000, Cr. Cash A/c ₹70,000. The cash distribution matches each partner's credit balance exactly, closing both the Capital Accounts and the Cash Account simultaneously at dissolution.

questions.question: If a partner takes over a firm's asset at BOOK VALUE during dissolution, the net effect on Realisation Account's profit or loss is:
options: Increases profit by the book value of asset
Decreases profit by the book value of asset
No effect on profit or loss
Creates a separate capital gain for the firm
questions.explanation: When asset taken at book value: Dr. Partner's Capital A/c (book value), Cr. Realisation A/c (book value). Asset was already debited to Realisation at book value (Step 1). Now the credit to Realisation is also at book value. Net effect: DR (book value from Step 1) = CR (book value from partner takeover) → ZERO net impact on Realisation profit/loss. This is why book value takeover is considered 'neutral.' Any difference between agreed price and book value would create profit (agreed > BV) or loss (agreed < BV) in Realisation Account.

questions.question: Which of the following does NOT appear in the Realisation Account at dissolution?
options: Sundry Debtors
Provision for Bad and Doubtful Debts
Partners' Capital Accounts
Bills Payable
questions.explanation: Partners' Capital Accounts do NOT appear in Realisation Account. Realisation Account contains: (Debit) all tangible assets at BV, actual payments to settle liabilities, realisation expenses, liabilities assumed by partners; (Credit) all outside liabilities at BV, cash proceeds from assets, assets taken over by partners. After Realisation Account is balanced, the profit/loss is transferred to Partners' Capital Accounts — but the Capital Accounts themselves are separate ledger accounts, not part of Realisation Account.

questions.question: At dissolution, Creditors of ₹36,000 accept ₹33,000 in full settlement. Partners share profits 2:1. What is the gain from creditors, and each partner's share of this gain?
options: Gain ₹33,000; shared equally
questions.explanation: Creditors BV = ₹36,000 (transferred to CR of Realisation). Amount paid = ₹33,000 (Dr. Realisation, Cr. Cash). Net on Realisation from creditors: CR ₹36,000 − DR ₹33,000 = CR ₹3,000 = Gain of ₹3,000. This gain forms part of the overall Realisation profit/loss and is distributed in PSR (2:1). A's share = 3,000 × 2/3 = ₹2,000; B's share = 3,000 × 1/3 = ₹1,000. The ₹3,000 saving on settling creditors benefits all partners in their profit-sharing ratio — it is not kept by the firm separately.

questions.question: Goodwill appearing in the Balance Sheet at book value ₹30,000 at the time of dissolution is:
options: Written off directly against Partners' Capital Accounts before Realisation entries
Transferred to the Debit side of Realisation Account like other assets
Shown only on the credit side if it can be sold
Ignored as goodwill has no physical form
questions.explanation: If Goodwill appears in the Balance Sheet (has been recorded), it is an intangible asset and must be transferred to Realisation Account: Dr. Realisation A/c ₹30,000, Cr. Goodwill A/c ₹30,000. If sold: Dr. Cash A/c (proceeds), Cr. Realisation A/c. If not sold: Realisation Account bears the loss of ₹30,000 which flows to partners in PSR. Goodwill is treated exactly like any other asset — transferred at BV to Realisation's debit side regardless of whether it will actually be sold. Writing off against capital is incorrect if it appears in the Balance Sheet.

questions.question: Which of the following is a ground for court-ordered dissolution of a firm under Section 44 of the Indian Partnership Act, 1932?
options: All partners agree unanimously to dissolve
One partner retires voluntarily
A partner becomes permanently incapable of performing his duties as a partner
The firm completes its agreed duration
questions.explanation: Under Section 44, a court may dissolve a firm if a partner has become of permanently incapable mind or body, making him permanently unable to perform his partnership duties. Other grounds include: insanity, misconduct affecting business, persistent breach of the partnership deed, transfer of entire interest to a third party, business operating at a continuous loss, and any just and equitable ground. Options A (mutual agreement) and D (expiry of period) are grounds for dissolution without court intervention. Option B (voluntary retirement) leads to dissolution of partnership, not firm.

questions.question: X and Y share profits in 3:2. Realisation loss = ₹25,000. Y's capital before adjusting loss = ₹18,000 (Cr). Y's capital AFTER adjusting the Realisation loss is:
questions.explanation: Y's share of Realisation loss = ₹25,000 × 2/5 = ₹10,000. Y's Capital A/c: Opening Cr ₹18,000 − Loss share Dr ₹10,000 = ₹8,000 (Cr). Y's capital shows a credit balance of ₹8,000, meaning the firm owes Y ₹8,000. Y will receive ₹8,000 in cash at final settlement. X's share = ₹25,000 × 3/5 = ₹15,000. If Y's capital had been less than ₹10,000, the result would be a debit balance, indicating Y must pay the firm — potentially triggering insolvency considerations.

questions.question: An unrecorded outstanding liability (employee bonus of ₹8,000 not in books) is paid in cash during dissolution. The correct journal entry is:
options: Dr. Bonus Expense A/c ₹8,000; Cr. Cash A/c ₹8,000
Dr. Realisation A/c ₹8,000; Cr. Cash/Bank A/c ₹8,000
Dr. Partners' Capital A/cs ₹8,000; Cr. Cash A/c ₹8,000
No entry needed as it was not in the books
questions.explanation: An unrecorded liability has no corresponding account in the books. When discovered and paid at dissolution, it is recorded directly in Realisation Account: Dr. Realisation A/c ₹8,000, Cr. Cash/Bank A/c ₹8,000. This increases the debit side of Realisation Account, reducing profit or increasing loss. The impact is ultimately shared by partners in PSR through the final profit/loss distribution. The bonus account does not need to be created separately — the direct Realisation entry captures the cash outflow and automatically adjusts the dissolution outcome.

questions.question: Investments held by the firm at dissolution are:
options: Transferred to Partners' Capital Accounts directly in PSR
Transferred to the Debit side of Realisation Account at book value
Not treated as assets and kept outside Realisation Account
Set off against any related loans taken for investments
questions.explanation: Investments are assets of the firm — they are transferred to the Debit side of Realisation Account at book value, just like any other asset: Dr. Realisation A/c, Cr. Investment A/c. Upon sale: Dr. Cash/Bank A/c (proceeds), Cr. Realisation A/c. If a partner takes over investments: Dr. Partner's Capital A/c, Cr. Realisation A/c (at agreed price). Investments are NOT distributed directly to partners in PSR — that would be incorrect. They must pass through Realisation Account to determine the profit or loss on their disposal.

questions.question: A firm has: Opening Cash ₹8,000; Assets (excluding cash) realized ₹1,65,000; Creditors ₹70,000 paid in full; Bills Payable ₹20,000 paid in full; Realisation expenses ₹3,000. Cash available for distribution to partners is:
questions.explanation: Total cash inflow = Opening cash (8,000) + Realisation proceeds (1,65,000) = ₹1,73,000. Total payments = Creditors (70,000) + Bills Payable (20,000) + Expenses (3,000) = ₹93,000. Cash available for partners = 1,73,000 − 93,000 = ₹80,000. This amount is used to: first repay any partners' loans, then repay partners' capital balances. Partners' Capital Accounts (after adjusting Realisation profit/loss) should total ₹80,000 for the accounts to balance.

questions.question: Partners' drawings during the dissolution year are:
options: Transferred to Realisation Account as an expense
Adjusted against Partners' Capital Accounts as a normal accounting entry
Recovered from the firm's realisation proceeds before any distribution
Forgiven automatically at the time of dissolution
questions.explanation: Partners' drawings are a personal transaction between the partner and the firm, reducing the partner's capital. Before dissolution entries are passed, all normal accounting entries for the year (including closing Drawings A/c to Capital A/c) should be completed: Dr. Partner's Capital A/c, Cr. Drawings A/c. This reduces the partner's capital balance. Drawings are NOT transferred to Realisation Account — they have no connection with asset realisation or liability settlement. The Capital Account balance after closing drawings is the starting point for dissolution capital account workings.

questions.question: A partner has advanced a loan of ₹60,000 to the firm. After paying all outside creditors, only ₹35,000 cash remains. How is the partner's loan treated?
options: Partner receives ₹60,000; shortfall is treated as capital contribution
Partner receives ₹35,000; remaining ₹25,000 is absorbed equally by all partners as a dissolution loss
Partner's loan is adjusted against his capital account fully
Partner waives ₹25,000 as it cannot be paid
questions.explanation: Partner's loan of ₹60,000 is payable from remaining cash after outside creditors. Only ₹35,000 is available. Partner receives ₹35,000, and the remaining ₹25,000 shortfall on the loan becomes an irrecoverable amount. This ₹25,000 must be treated as a loss shared by ALL partners in PSR — it is a business loss arising from insufficient funds, not solely the lender-partner's burden. If the firm is technically insolvent (assets insufficient to cover all liabilities including loans), partners collectively bear the loss.

questions.question: Prepaid Insurance (₹5,000) appearing as an asset in the Balance Sheet at dissolution is:
options: Refunded by the insurance company automatically
Transferred to the Debit side of Realisation Account at book value
Written off against Partners' Capital Accounts in PSR
Carried forward to the next accounting period
questions.explanation: Prepaid Insurance is a current asset — an advance payment for a service not yet utilized. At dissolution, it is transferred to the Debit side of Realisation Account: Dr. Realisation A/c ₹5,000, Cr. Prepaid Insurance A/c ₹5,000. If the insurance company refunds any amount, it is credited to Realisation Account. If nothing is recovered, the ₹5,000 becomes part of the realisation loss. It cannot be carried forward (no future period) and is not written off against capital (it has some potential realizable value, unlike fictitious assets).

questions.question: M and N share profits in 2:3. Assets (excluding cash): Machinery ₹80,000, Debtors ₹40,000, Stock ₹30,000. Liabilities: Creditors ₹60,000. M takes over Machinery at ₹72,000. Debtors realized ₹36,000. Stock sold for ₹26,000. Creditors settled for ₹57,000. Realisation expenses ₹2,000. The Profit/Loss on Realisation is:
options: Loss of ₹5,000
Profit of ₹5,000
Loss of ₹15,000
Profit of ₹15,000
questions.explanation: Debit side of Realisation = assets ₹1,50,000 + creditors paid ₹57,000 + expenses ₹2,000 = ₹2,09,000. Credit side = creditors ₹60,000 + machinery taken by M ₹72,000 + debtors realised ₹36,000 + stock realised ₹26,000 = ₹1,94,000. Debit exceeds credit by ₹15,000, so there is a Realisation loss of ₹15,000.

questions.question: Partners Raj and Rohit share profits equally. Assets (excl. cash) BV ₹2,50,000, realized ₹2,30,000. Liabilities BV ₹1,00,000, paid ₹90,000. Realisation expenses ₹5,000. Each partner's share of Realisation loss is:
options: ₹7,500 each
₹5,000 each
₹2,500 each
questions.explanation: DR Realisation: Assets BV (2,50,000) + Liabilities paid (90,000) + Expenses (5,000) = ₹3,45,000. CR Realisation: Liabilities BV (1,00,000) + Cash realized (2,30,000) = ₹3,30,000. Total Loss = 3,45,000 − 3,30,000 = ₹15,000. Each partner's share (equal ratio 1:1) = ₹15,000 ÷ 2 = ₹7,500 each. Alternatively: Asset loss (20,000) − Liability gain (10,000) + Expenses (5,000) = ₹15,000 net loss. Each partner bears ₹7,500, which is debited to their respective Capital Accounts.

questions.question: An UNRECORDED investment (not in books) is discovered at dissolution and taken over by Partner B at ₹9,000. The correct journal entry is:
options: Dr. Realisation A/c; Cr. Investment A/c ₹9,000; then Dr. B's Capital ₹9,000; Cr. Realisation ₹9,000
Dr. B's Capital A/c ₹9,000; Cr. Realisation A/c ₹9,000
Dr. B's Capital A/c ₹9,000; Cr. Partners' Capital A/cs ₹9,000
Dr. Cash A/c ₹9,000; Cr. Realisation A/c ₹9,000
questions.explanation: An unrecorded asset has NO book entry — there is no Investment Account to credit. Therefore, no Step 1 transfer entry is needed. When taken over by Partner B at ₹9,000: Dr. B's Capital A/c ₹9,000, Cr. Realisation A/c ₹9,000. Realisation Account is credited at the agreed value (₹9,000) as if the asset was 'sold' to B at that price. This increases the credit side of Realisation Account, contributing to profit (or reducing loss). The absence of a book value means there is no comparison loss/gain — only the realized consideration enters the accounts.

questions.question: A and B (PSR 3:2) dissolve their firm. Balance Sheet: Creditors ₹25,000, Bills Payable ₹15,000, A's Capital ₹60,000, B's Capital ₹40,000. Assets: Machinery ₹50,000, Debtors ₹40,000, Stock ₹30,000, Cash ₹20,000. A took Machinery at ₹45,000. All Debtors realized. Bills Payable paid fully. Creditors accepted ₹23,000 in full settlement. Stock realized ₹25,000. Expenses ₹2,000. Profit/Loss on Realisation:
options: Loss of ₹8,000
Profit of ₹10,000
Loss of ₹10,000
Profit of ₹8,000
questions.explanation: DR: Assets BV (50+40+30 = 1,20,000) + Bills Payable paid (15,000) + Creditors paid (23,000) + Expenses (2,000) = ₹1,60,000. CR: Creditors BV (25,000) + Bills Payable BV (15,000) + Cash: Debtors (40,000) + Stock (25,000) + A's Capital for Machinery (45,000) = ₹1,50,000. Loss = 1,60,000 − 1,50,000 = ₹10,000. A's share = 10,000 × 3/5 = ₹6,000; B's share = ₹4,000. The gain on creditor settlement (₹2,000) partially offsets the losses on machinery and stock.

questions.question: From the Q44 scenario: A and B (3:2), Realisation Loss ₹10,000. A's Capital before dissolution adjustments = ₹60,000 (Cr). A took over Machinery at ₹45,000. A's final capital balance (cash to be received) is:
questions.explanation: A's Capital A/c workings: Opening Cr ₹60,000. Less: Machinery taken at ₹45,000 (Dr. A's Capital, Cr. Realisation): −₹45,000 → ₹15,000. Less: Share of Realisation loss (3/5 × ₹10,000 = ₹6,000): −₹6,000 → ₹9,000 (Cr). A receives ₹9,000 in cash. Entry: Dr. A's Capital ₹9,000, Cr. Cash A/c ₹9,000. B's Capital: 40,000 − 4,000 (loss) = ₹36,000 (Cr). Total distribution = 9,000 + 36,000 = ₹45,000 from remaining cash.

questions.question: At dissolution, Debtors = ₹60,000 and Provision for Bad Debts (PBD) = ₹4,000. How are these transferred to Realisation Account?
options: Dr. Realisation A/c ₹56,000 (net); Cr. Debtors A/c ₹56,000
Dr. Realisation A/c ₹60,000; Cr. Debtors A/c ₹60,000; AND Dr. PBD A/c ₹4,000; Cr. Realisation A/c ₹4,000
Dr. Realisation A/c ₹64,000; Cr. Debtors ₹60,000; Cr. PBD ₹4,000
Dr. Debtors A/c ₹60,000; Cr. Realisation A/c ₹60,000 only
questions.explanation: Debtors (asset) transferred at gross value: Dr. Realisation A/c ₹60,000, Cr. Debtors A/c ₹60,000. PBD (contra asset / credit balance account) transferred like a liability: Dr. PBD A/c ₹4,000, Cr. Realisation A/c ₹4,000. Net effect on Realisation = DR ₹60,000 − CR ₹4,000 = net DR ₹56,000 for debtors group. When cash is actually collected (say ₹53,000 after bad debts): Dr. Cash ₹53,000, Cr. Realisation ₹53,000. Net loss on debtors = 60,000 − 4,000 − 53,000 = ₹3,000.

questions.question: Workmen Compensation Reserve (WCR) = ₹20,000. Actual claim settled = ₹12,000. How is the WCR treated at dissolution?
options: Entire ₹20,000 transferred to Realisation Account credit side
₹12,000 paid from cash (Dr. WCR, Cr. Cash); remaining ₹8,000 credited to Partners' Capitals in PSR
₹20,000 credited to Partners' Capitals first; then ₹12,000 paid from remaining cash
The full ₹20,000 is used to pay creditors before partners
questions.explanation: WCR is a specific-purpose reserve. Step 1: Settle the actual claim: Dr. WCR A/c ₹12,000, Cr. Cash A/c ₹12,000. Step 2: Transfer excess to Partners' Capitals: Dr. WCR A/c ₹8,000, Cr. Partners' Capital A/cs ₹8,000 (in PSR). The excess ₹8,000 (beyond the claim) is not needed for the purpose and reverts to partners as undistributed profit. This treatment ensures the reserve serves its intended purpose first. Option C creates timing issues — credits all ₹20,000 to capital first, then the claim payment later would create an anomaly.

questions.question: When a partner agrees to take over (discharge) a firm's outside liability, the entry after the liability has already been transferred to Realisation Account's credit side is:
options: Dr. Liability A/c; Cr. Cash A/c (cash paid by partner)
Dr. Realisation A/c; Cr. Partner's Capital A/c (at amount of liability)
Dr. Partner's Capital A/c; Cr. Realisation A/c
No additional entry required
questions.explanation: Liability already on CR of Realisation (Step 2). When a partner discharges it: the firm doesn't pay cash — the partner does from personal funds. Entry: Dr. Realisation A/c (at amount discharged), Cr. Partner's Capital A/c. This records: (1) Realisation A/c bears the outflow (as if firm paid), and (2) Partner's Capital A/c is credited — the firm now owes the partner this amount (his capital increases). Net on Realisation: CR (liability BV from Step 2) vs DR (actual amount discharged). If partner paid full liability at BV: net zero impact. If paid less: gain for firm.

questions.question: Partners X and Y share profits equally. At dissolution: Machinery (BV ₹60,000) sold for ₹55,000; Debtors (BV ₹30,000) — ₹2,000 irrecoverable, balance recovered; Stock (BV ₹20,000) sold ₹22,000; Furniture (BV ₹8,000) taken by X at BV. Creditors ₹25,000 fully paid. Expenses ₹1,500. Profit/Loss on Realisation:
options: Loss of ₹6,500
Profit of ₹6,500
Loss of ₹4,500
Profit of ₹4,500
questions.explanation: DR Realisation: Assets BV (60+30+20+8 = ₹1,18,000) + Creditors paid (25,000) + Expenses (1,500) = ₹1,44,500. CR Realisation: Creditors BV (25,000) + Cash: Machinery (55,000) + Debtors (28,000) + Stock (22,000) + X's Capital for Furniture (8,000) = ₹1,38,000. Loss = 1,44,500 − 1,38,000 = ₹6,500. Note: Furniture taken at BV has zero impact (DR and CR both ₹8,000 cancel). Debtors recovered = 30,000 − 2,000 = ₹28,000. Loss shared equally: ₹3,250 each.

questions.question: P, Q, R are partners sharing profits in 2:2:1. At dissolution, General Reserve = ₹50,000. Before Realisation Account, General Reserve is distributed. How much does R receive from General Reserve?
questions.explanation: General Reserve is distributed in profit-sharing ratio (2:2:1) before Realisation Account is prepared. Total PSR = 5. R's share = 50,000 × 1/5 = ₹10,000. P's share = 50,000 × 2/5 = ₹20,000. Q's share = ₹20,000. Total = 10,000 + 20,000 + 20,000 = ₹50,000 ✓. Entry for R: Dr. General Reserve A/c ₹10,000, Cr. R's Capital A/c ₹10,000. This increases R's capital balance by ₹10,000 before any dissolution adjustments are recorded.

questions.question: Partners A and B (3:2). Capitals: A = ₹80,000 (Cr), B = ₹50,000 (Cr). General Reserve ₹20,000. Realisation loss ₹30,000. A took Stock at ₹18,000 (already factored in loss). B paid expenses ₹5,000 from personal funds (factored in loss). Find A's final capital balance:
questions.explanation: Step 1 — Distribute GR: A gets 3/5 × 20,000 = ₹12,000 (Cr). A's capital = 80,000 + 12,000 = ₹92,000. Step 2 — A takes Stock at ₹18,000: Dr. A's Capital ₹18,000. A's capital = ₹74,000. Step 3 — Realisation loss share: A's share = 3/5 × 30,000 = ₹18,000 (Dr). A's capital = 74,000 − 18,000 = ₹56,000 (Cr). A receives ₹56,000 in cash. B's expenses paid from personal funds (Cr. B's Capital ₹5,000) affect B's account only. A's account is unaffected by B's actions.

questions.question: Assertion (A): Cash and Bank Balances are NOT transferred to the Realisation Account at the time of dissolution. Reason (R): Cash and Bank are liquid assets already available and can be directly used for payments without being routed through Realisation Account.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true, but R is false
A is false, but R is true
questions.explanation: Both Assertion and Reason are correct, and R accurately explains A. The Realisation Account's purpose is to convert non-liquid assets into cash by recording their sale proceeds. Since Cash/Bank is already in liquid form, routing it through Realisation Account serves no accounting purpose. Cash remains in the Cash Account and is used directly: to pay outside creditors, reimburse partners' loans, pay realisation expenses, and finally distribute to partners. The Realisation Account only closes non-cash assets and liabilities.

questions.question: A firm has Goodwill in books at ₹50,000. At dissolution, goodwill is sold for ₹35,000. The net impact on Realisation Account from this single asset is:
options: Profit of ₹15,000 (credit)
Loss of ₹15,000 (net debit of ₹15,000 on Realisation)
Loss of ₹50,000 (full book value lost)
No impact as goodwill is intangible
questions.explanation: Step 1: Transfer Goodwill to Realisation at BV: Dr. Realisation ₹50,000, Cr. Goodwill A/c ₹50,000 (Realisation DR +50,000). Step 3: Cash received: Dr. Cash ₹35,000, Cr. Realisation ₹35,000 (Realisation CR +35,000). Net impact on Realisation from Goodwill: DR ₹50,000 − CR ₹35,000 = Net DR ₹15,000 = Loss of ₹15,000. This loss is part of overall Realisation loss, shared in PSR. Option C is wrong — the loss is only ₹15,000 (difference between BV and realized value), not the full BV of ₹50,000.

questions.question: At dissolution, Creditors (₹50,000) agree to take over Plant (BV ₹40,000, agreed value ₹45,000), with the remaining balance paid in cash. How much cash does the firm actually pay to the creditors?
questions.explanation: Creditors are owed ₹50,000. They accept Plant valued at ₹45,000 (agreed). This reduces their claim by ₹45,000. Remaining amount payable in cash = ₹50,000 − ₹45,000 = ₹5,000. The Realisation Account shows: CR from creditors BV (50,000); DR from creditors paid = ₹5,000 cash + plant at agreed ₹45,000 effectively passes through. Net gain on creditor settlement = 0 (fully settled). Plant generates: CR ₹45,000 to Realisation vs DR ₹40,000 (BV) = Profit ₹5,000 on Plant. Only ₹5,000 cash flows out.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a correct statement about the Realisation Account?
options: It determines the profit or loss arising from the process of dissolution
All assets excluding Cash and Bank are debited at their book values
Partners' Loans to the firm are transferred to its Credit side like other liabilities
The closing balance of Realisation Account is transferred to Partners' Capital Accounts
questions.explanation: Partners' Loans are NOT transferred to Realisation Account — this is a factually incorrect statement. Partners' loans are a separate category of obligation, paid after outside creditors but before capital. They are settled directly: Dr. Partner's Loan A/c, Cr. Cash A/c. They do not go through Realisation Account. All other options are correct: Realisation A/c determines P/L on dissolution (A), all non-cash assets are debited at BV (B), and the closing balance (profit/loss) is credited/debited to Partners' Capital Accounts (D).

questions.question: Partners E and F (2:1). E takes over Debtors (BV ₹30,000) at ₹28,000. F discharges Creditors (BV ₹20,000) paying only ₹18,000 from personal funds (creditors grant discount). The combined net effect on Realisation Account profit/loss from these two transactions:
options: Loss of ₹2,000
Profit of ₹2,000
No net effect (break-even)
Loss of ₹4,000
questions.explanation: Effect from Debtors: Realisation DR ₹30,000 (BV) vs Realisation CR ₹28,000 (E's Capital) = Net DR ₹2,000 = Loss of ₹2,000. Effect from Creditors: Realisation CR ₹20,000 (BV) vs Realisation DR ₹18,000 (F paid, so Dr. Realisation, Cr. F's Capital) = Net CR ₹2,000 = Gain of ₹2,000. Combined: Loss ₹2,000 + Gain ₹2,000 = ZERO net effect. The ₹2,000 loss on debtors exactly cancels the ₹2,000 gain on creditors in this scenario. The overall Realisation profit/loss is unaffected by these two transactions in combination.

questions.question: A and B are equal partners. Journal entries at dissolution: (1) Dr. Realisation ₹1,50,000; Cr. Various Assets ₹1,50,000. (2) Dr. Creditors ₹40,000; Cr. Realisation ₹40,000. (3) Dr. Bank ₹1,30,000; Cr. Realisation ₹1,30,000. (4) Dr. Realisation ₹40,000; Cr. Bank ₹40,000. (5) Dr. Realisation ₹3,000; Cr. Bank ₹3,000. (6) Dr. A's Capital ₹25,000; Cr. Realisation ₹25,000. What is the Profit/Loss and A's share?
options: Loss of ₹23,000; A's share = ₹11,500
Profit of ₹2,000; A's share = ₹1,000
Loss of ₹2,000; A's share = ₹1,000
Profit of ₹12,000; A's share = ₹6,000
questions.explanation: DR Realisation: Entry 1 (1,50,000) + Entry 4 (40,000) + Entry 5 (3,000) = ₹1,93,000. CR Realisation: Entry 2 (40,000) + Entry 3 (1,30,000) + Entry 6 (25,000) = ₹1,95,000. CR > DR → Profit = ₹2,000. A's share (equal) = ₹1,000. Entry 4 records actual cash paid to creditors (BV already on CR from Entry 2 — same amount, so no gain/loss on creditors). Entry 6 = A took asset at ₹25,000 (CR Realisation). Final entry for profit: Dr. Realisation ₹2,000; Cr. A's Capital ₹1,000; Cr. B's Capital ₹1,000.

questions.question: Which of the following is a CREDIT item in the Realisation Account arising from the dissolution Balance Sheet?
options: Machinery (tangible asset)
Bills Receivable (current asset)
Outstanding Salaries to Employees (outside liability)
Prepaid Expenses (current asset)
questions.explanation: Outstanding Salaries to Employees is an outside liability (firm owes money to employees). When transferred to Realisation Account, liabilities are DEBITED (closed) and Realisation A/c is CREDITED: Dr. Outstanding Salaries A/c, Cr. Realisation A/c. All assets (Machinery, Bills Receivable, Prepaid Expenses) generate DEBIT entries in Realisation Account when transferred. Only liabilities appear on the credit side of Realisation Account when transferred. When the outstanding salary is actually paid: Dr. Realisation A/c, Cr. Cash A/c (debit Realisation).

questions.question: Partner A has advanced a loan of ₹20,000 to the firm. A's Capital = ₹50,000 (Cr). Cash available after paying all outside creditors = ₹75,000. Partner B's Capital = ₹5,000 (Cr). What is the total amount received by Partner A?
questions.explanation: Sequence per Section 48: (1) Outside creditors paid (done) — ₹75,000 remains. (2) Partner's Loan paid: Dr. A's Loan A/c ₹20,000, Cr. Cash ₹20,000 → Cash left = ₹55,000. (3) Partners' Capital: A receives ₹50,000, B receives ₹5,000 → Total = ₹55,000 ✓. A's total receipt = Loan (₹20,000) + Capital (₹50,000) = ₹70,000. B receives ₹5,000. Total cash distributed = 20,000 + 50,000 + 5,000 = ₹75,000 ✓. Partner's loan is repaid separately before capital — it is NOT merged with capital.

questions.question: Assertion (A): Realisation profit or loss is always distributed among partners in their capital ratio. Reason (R): Partners' capital contributions determine their share in the profits and losses of the firm.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is false; R is true (with qualification)
Both A and R are false
questions.explanation: Assertion is FALSE — Realisation profit/loss is distributed in PROFIT-SHARING RATIO (PSR), NOT capital ratio. Capital ratio is used for interest on capital and under Garner v Murray rule for capital deficiency due to insolvency. Reason R is also imprecise — while capital contributions are a factor, the PSR (which may differ significantly from capital ratio) governs all profit/loss sharing including realisation outcome. PSR is agreed in the partnership deed and may be equal or in any proportion, irrespective of capital contributions. Hence, A is false and R is partially misleading.

questions.question: R and S share profits 3:2. Balance Sheet on dissolution: Creditors ₹40,000, BP ₹20,000, R's Capital ₹70,000, S's Capital ₹50,000. Assets: Land ₹60,000, Plant ₹50,000, Stock ₹30,000, Debtors ₹25,000, Cash ₹15,000. Land realized ₹80,000; Plant taken by R at ₹45,000; Stock ₹28,000; ₹3,000 debtors bad, rest realized; BP paid fully; Creditors settled ₹38,000; Expenses ₹2,000. R's share of Realisation profit is:
questions.explanation: DR Realisation: Assets BV (165,000) + [BP (20,000) + Creditors (38,000) + Expenses (2,000)] = ₹2,25,000. CR Realisation: Liabilities BV [Creditors (40,000) + BP (20,000)] + Cash [Land (80,000) + Stock (28,000) + Debtors (22,000)] + R's Capital for Plant (45,000) = ₹2,35,000. Profit = 2,35,000 − 2,25,000 = ₹10,000. R's share (3/5) = ₹6,000; S's share (2/5) = ₹4,000. Debtors realized = 25,000 − 3,000 = ₹22,000.

questions.question: Continuing from Q61 scenario: R's Capital opening = ₹70,000 (Cr). Realisation profit = ₹10,000 (R's share = ₹6,000). R took Plant at ₹45,000. R's final capital balance (cash received at dissolution) is:
questions.explanation: R's Capital A/c: Opening Cr ₹70,000. Less: Plant taken at ₹45,000 (Dr): −₹45,000 → ₹25,000. Add: Realisation profit share (3/5 × 10,000): +₹6,000 → ₹31,000 (Cr). R receives ₹31,000 in cash. Entry: Dr. R's Capital A/c ₹31,000, Cr. Cash A/c ₹31,000. S's Capital: 50,000 + 4,000 (profit) = ₹54,000 (Cr). Total = 31,000 + 54,000 = ₹85,000, which equals total cash available for partners.

questions.question: In Q61's scenario, total cash available for distribution between R and S (after paying all outside liabilities) is:
questions.explanation: Cash inflows: Opening cash (15,000) + Land (80,000) + Stock (28,000) + Debtors (22,000) = ₹1,45,000. Cash outflows: BP (20,000) + Creditors (38,000) + Expenses (2,000) = ₹60,000. Cash available = 1,45,000 − 60,000 = ₹85,000. Verification: R receives ₹31,000 + S receives ₹54,000 = ₹85,000 ✓. Note: Plant does not generate cash (taken by R at ₹45,000 — adjusted in R's Capital). Cash Account perfectly balances, confirming accuracy of dissolution entries.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT an occasion for dissolution of the FIRM (as distinct from dissolution of partnership)?
options: All partners unanimously agree to dissolve
Adjudication of all partners as insolvent
Change in profit-sharing ratio among existing partners
The firm's business becomes unlawful under any law
questions.explanation: Change in profit-sharing ratio among existing partners results in dissolution of PARTNERSHIP (reconstitution of the old partnership), NOT dissolution of the FIRM. The firm continues operating; only the internal arrangement changes. This requires a new partnership deed. Options A (voluntary mutual agreement), B (compulsory — insolvency of all partners), and D (compulsory — illegal business) all lead to dissolution of the FIRM itself, requiring complete winding up, preparation of Realisation Account, and distribution to partners.

questions.question: An unrecorded patent (no book entry) is discovered at dissolution and is taken over by Partner K at ₹12,000. The correct journal entry is:
options: Dr. Patent A/c ₹12,000; Cr. Realisation A/c ₹12,000; then Dr. K's Capital ₹12,000; Cr. Realisation ₹12,000
Dr. K's Capital A/c ₹12,000; Cr. Realisation A/c ₹12,000
Dr. K's Capital A/c ₹12,000; Cr. Patent A/c ₹12,000
Dr. Realisation A/c ₹12,000; Cr. K's Capital ₹12,000
questions.explanation: Since the patent is UNRECORDED (no book entry), there is no Patent Account to create or close. There is no Step 1 transfer needed. When K takes over the unrecorded asset at ₹12,000: Dr. K's Capital A/c ₹12,000, Cr. Realisation A/c ₹12,000 — a single entry. Realisation Account is credited at ₹12,000 (value received for the asset), increasing the credit side and contributing to profit (or reducing loss). Since there is no BV, there is no comparison or gain/loss calculation — only the realized amount matters.

questions.question: In COMPLETE (non-piecemeal) dissolution, cash is distributed to partners:
options: As soon as each asset is individually realized
Only after ALL assets are realized and ALL outside liabilities (including partners' loans) are settled
In proportional installments as cash comes in
Partners withdraw first from the opening Cash balance
questions.explanation: In complete dissolution (standard Class 12 treatment), partners receive their final payment ONLY AFTER all assets are realized, all outside liabilities are paid, all partners' loans are repaid, and Realisation Account is closed with profit/loss distributed to Capital Accounts. No intermediate distribution is made. The Cash Account is maintained throughout, and only after all entries are complete are the Partners' Capital Account balances settled by a single payment to each partner. This contrasts with piecemeal distribution (not in Class 12 scope) where payments are made as cash becomes available.

questions.question: At dissolution, Investments (BV ₹80,000) sold for ₹70,000. Investment Fluctuation Fund (IFF) = ₹15,000. IFF is transferred to Realisation Account (credit side). The net profit or loss on Realisation from Investments + IFF combined is:
options: Loss of ₹10,000
Loss of ₹5,000
Profit of ₹5,000
No net effect
questions.explanation: In Realisation Account: DR side receives Investments at BV = ₹80,000. CR side receives IFF = ₹15,000 (Dr. IFF A/c, Cr. Realisation A/c) AND Cash from sale = ₹70,000. Total CR from this group = ₹85,000. Net: CR (85,000) > DR (80,000) = Profit of ₹5,000. Even though investments were sold at a ₹10,000 loss, the IFF of ₹15,000 more than covers it, creating a net ₹5,000 profit. This ₹5,000 is included in overall Realisation profit and shared in PSR.

questions.question: If an asset remains UNSOLD at the time of final settlement during dissolution, it should be:
options: Treated as a gift to the most senior partner
Written off against the Realisation Account at zero value
Distributed among partners at an agreed value, or sold to an outside party before closure
Entered as a contingent asset in final accounts
questions.explanation: At dissolution, ALL assets must be disposed of. No asset can remain in a dissolved firm's books. If an asset is unsold, partners must either: (a) agree to distribute it among themselves at a mutually agreed value (Dr. Partner's Capital A/cs, Cr. Realisation A/c), or (b) sell it to an outside party. Writing off at zero is only appropriate for worthless assets (e.g., completely obsolete equipment). The Realisation Account must capture every asset's disposal — either through cash proceeds or partner takeover at agreed values.

questions.question: A partner agrees to bear all Realisation expenses. Actual expenses = ₹10,000, paid by the partner from personal funds. The entry in Realisation Account is:
options: Dr. Realisation A/c ₹10,000; Cr. Cash A/c ₹10,000
Dr. Realisation A/c ₹10,000; Cr. Partner's Capital A/c ₹10,000
No entry in Realisation Account (only Partner's Capital is debited)
Dr. Partner's Capital A/c ₹10,000; Cr. Realisation A/c ₹10,000
questions.explanation: When a partner pays Realisation expenses from personal funds: Dr. Realisation A/c ₹10,000, Cr. Partner's Capital A/c ₹10,000. Realisation A/c is debited — the expense reduces realisation profit (or increases loss) and is ultimately shared by all partners in PSR. Partner's Capital is credited because the partner has effectively paid on behalf of the firm, creating a firm's liability to the partner. This is different from firm's cash payment: Dr. Realisation A/c, Cr. Cash A/c — the credit changes based on who pays.

questions.question: P and Q share profits 2:1. Balance Sheet: Creditors ₹30,000, P's Capital ₹60,000, Q's Capital ₹30,000. Assets: Machinery ₹50,000, Stock ₹40,000, Cash ₹30,000. At dissolution: Machinery sold ₹55,000; Stock sold ₹30,000; Creditors paid in full; Realisation expenses ₹1,000. What is P's share of Realisation Loss?
questions.explanation: DR Realisation: Assets BV (50+40 = 90,000) + Creditors paid (30,000) + Expenses (1,000) = ₹1,21,000. CR Realisation: Creditors BV (30,000) + Cash: Machinery (55,000) + Stock (30,000) = ₹1,15,000. Loss = 1,21,000 − 1,15,000 = ₹6,000. P's share (2/3) = ₹4,000; Q's share (1/3) = ₹2,000. Loss arises from: Stock sold at loss (40,000 − 30,000 = ₹10,000 loss) − Machinery profit (55,000 − 50,000 = ₹5,000 gain) + Expenses (₹1,000) = ₹6,000 net loss.

questions.question: A, B, C share profits 3:2:1. At dissolution: Total assets (excl. cash) BV ₹4,20,000; Cash ₹30,000. Liabilities: Creditors ₹80,000, A's Loan ₹40,000. Capitals: A = ₹1,60,000, B = ₹1,00,000, C = ₹70,000. Assets realized ₹3,50,000. Creditors settled ₹78,000. Realisation expenses ₹4,000. C's final cash receipt (after all adjustments) is:
questions.explanation: Realisation loss = assets ₹4,20,000 + creditors paid ₹78,000 + expenses ₹4,000 - creditors ₹80,000 - assets realised ₹3,50,000 = ₹72,000. C bears 1/6 of the loss = ₹12,000. C’s final capital receipt = ₹70,000 - ₹12,000 = ₹58,000.

questions.question: At dissolution of X-Y (equal partners): Unrecorded creditor of ₹6,000 is discovered and paid; Unrecorded investment is recovered and sold for ₹9,000; An employee claims ₹4,000 (unrecorded liability) — settled at ₹3,500; A machine (BV ₹20,000) is handed over to a creditor (₹22,000 BV) in full settlement. Net effect of ALL four transactions on Realisation Account profit/loss:
options: Loss of ₹500
Profit of ₹1,500
Profit of ₹500
Profit of ₹2,500
questions.explanation: Unrecorded creditor paid causes a ₹6,000 loss. Unrecorded investment sold creates a ₹9,000 gain. Employee claim settled at ₹3,500 causes a ₹3,500 loss. Machine of ₹20,000 handed to a ₹22,000 creditor creates a net ₹2,000 gain. Net effect = -₹6,000 + ₹9,000 - ₹3,500 + ₹2,000 = ₹1,500 profit.

questions.question: P and Q share profits 3:2. Capitals: P = ₹1,20,000, Q = ₹80,000. General Reserve = ₹30,000. Accumulated Loss = ₹10,000. At dissolution: Realisation loss (before GR and loss adjustments) = ₹50,000. Q's final capital balance is:
questions.explanation: Q receives 2/5 of General Reserve = ₹12,000 and bears 2/5 of accumulated loss = ₹4,000. Q also bears 2/5 of Realisation loss = ₹20,000. Final capital = ₹80,000 + ₹12,000 - ₹4,000 - ₹20,000 = ₹68,000 credit.

questions.question: Partners A and B (2:1). Capitals: A = ₹90,000, B = ₹60,000. Creditors = ₹45,000. Assets (excl. cash): Plant ₹80,000, Debtors ₹60,000, Stock ₹35,000. Cash = ₹20,000. Dissolution: Plant→A at ₹70,000; Debtors→B at ₹54,000; Stock realized ₹40,000; Creditors settled ₹42,000; Expenses ₹3,000. Calculate the Realisation profit or loss:
options: Loss of ₹2,000
Profit of ₹6,000
Loss of ₹11,000
Profit of ₹2,000
questions.explanation: Debit side of Realisation = assets ₹1,75,000 + creditors paid ₹42,000 + expenses ₹3,000 = ₹2,20,000. Credit side = creditors ₹45,000 + plant taken by A ₹70,000 + debtors taken by B ₹54,000 + stock realised ₹40,000 = ₹2,09,000. Loss = ₹2,20,000 - ₹2,09,000 = ₹11,000.

questions.question: Assertion (A): Under Garner v Murray rule, a solvent partner must bear an insolvent partner's capital deficiency in their last agreed capital ratio, not profit-sharing ratio. Reason (R): The Garner v Murray rule is based on the principle that the loss of an insolvent partner's deficiency is a capital loss, borne in proportion to the relative capital contributions of solvent partners.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true, but R is partially misleading
A is false; R is true
questions.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A. Garner v Murray (1904) established: when a partner is insolvent (cannot pay his capital deficiency), the remaining solvent partners absorb the deficiency in their last agreed CAPITAL RATIO. The reasoning (as per R): this is a CAPITAL loss — the firm failed to recover a capital contribution, not a business profit/loss. Therefore, it should be shared proportionate to each solvent partner's capital stake, not their PSR. For example, if solvent partners' capitals are 3:1, they bear the deficiency 3:1 even if PSR is 1:1.

questions.question: A, B, C share profits equally. Capitals: A = ₹60,000, B = ₹40,000, C = ₹30,000. Realisation loss = ₹99,000. All partners share loss equally. C is insolvent and cannot pay anything. Under Garner v Murray rule, how much does A bear of C's deficiency?
questions.explanation: Realisation loss per partner = ₹99,000 / 3 = ₹33,000. C has capital of ₹30,000, so C’s deficiency is ₹3,000. Under Garner v Murray, solvent partners bear the deficiency in their capital ratio. A:B capital ratio = ₹60,000:₹40,000 = 3:2. A bears 3/5 x ₹3,000 = ₹1,800.

questions.question: P, Q share profits 2:3. Dissolution Balance Sheet: Assets ₹2,50,000, Cash ₹20,000. Liabilities: Creditors ₹70,000, P's Loan ₹30,000, Capitals: P = ₹1,00,000, Q = ₹70,000. Assets realized ₹2,10,000. Creditors paid ₹67,000. Expenses ₹3,000. Cash received by Q at the end of dissolution is:

Partners L, M, N (PSR 2:2:1). Capitals: L = ₹80,000, M = ₹60,000, N = ₹40,000. Realisation shows: Assets BV ₹2,80,000, realized ₹2,55,000; Liabilities BV ₹90,000, settled ₹85,000; Unrecorded liability paid ₹8,000; Unrecorded asset sold ₹5,000. Realisation loss/profit and each partner's share:
questions.explanation: DR Realisation: Assets BV (2,80,000) + Liabilities paid (85,000) + Unrecorded liability (8,000) = ₹3,73,000. CR Realisation: Liabilities BV (90,000) + Cash realized (2,55,000) + Unrecorded asset sold (5,000) = ₹3,50,000. Loss = 3,73,000 − 3,50,000 = ₹23,000. PSR 2:2:1. L = 23,000 × 2/5 = ₹9,200; M = ₹9,200; N = 23,000 × 1/5 = ₹4,600. The unrecorded items: liability increases DR (worsens outcome); asset increases CR (improves outcome). Net from unrecorded items: −8,000 + 5,000 = ₹3,000 additional loss integrated into Realisation.

questions.question: P and Q (3:2) dissolve their firm. Plant (BV ₹90,000) — P takes it at ₹80,000. Stock (BV ₹40,000) realized ₹45,000. Debtors (BV ₹30,000) — Q bears ₹3,000 bad debt, rest realized. Unrecorded liability ₹5,000 paid. Creditors (BV ₹50,000) settled ₹47,000. Expenses: Q paid ₹2,000 from personal funds. Calculate P's share of Realisation outcome (profit/loss):
options: Loss of ₹7,200
Profit of ₹6,000
Profit of ₹3,000
Loss of ₹3,000
questions.explanation: Plant loss = ₹10,000, stock gain = ₹5,000, debtors loss = ₹3,000, creditor saving = ₹3,000, unrecorded liability = ₹5,000 loss, and expenses paid by Q = ₹2,000 loss. Net Realisation loss = ₹12,000. P bears 3/5 of the loss = ₹7,200.

questions.question: At dissolution, Provision for Bad Debts (PBD) = ₹5,000. Debtors = ₹45,000. Debtors realize ₹38,000. Which combination of entries correctly handles Debtors and PBD in Realisation Account?
options: Dr. Realisation ₹45,000; Cr. Debtors ₹45,000; Dr. PBD ₹5,000; Cr. Realisation ₹5,000; Dr. Cash ₹38,000; Cr. Realisation ₹38,000
Dr. Realisation ₹40,000 (net); Cr. Debtors ₹40,000; Dr. Cash ₹38,000; Cr. Realisation ₹38,000
Dr. Realisation ₹45,000; Cr. Debtors ₹45,000; Dr. Cash ₹38,000; Cr. Realisation ₹43,000; Cr. Loss on Debtors ₹2,000
Dr. Debtors ₹45,000; Cr. Realisation ₹45,000 first
questions.explanation: Step 1: Transfer Debtors at GROSS value to Realisation: Dr. Realisation ₹45,000, Cr. Debtors ₹45,000. Step 2: Transfer PBD (credit balance) to Realisation credit: Dr. PBD ₹5,000, Cr. Realisation ₹5,000. Step 3: Record cash collected: Dr. Cash ₹38,000, Cr. Realisation ₹38,000. Net on Realisation from debtors group: DR 45,000 − CR (5,000 + 38,000 = 43,000) = Net DR ₹2,000 = Loss of ₹2,000 on debtors. Option B is technically workable but non-standard (wrong to show net debtors). Option A correctly uses the standard 3-entry approach.

questions.question: W and V share profits 5:3. At dissolution: Balance Sheet shows — Capitals: W ₹1,50,000, V ₹90,000; Creditors ₹70,000; V's Loan ₹25,000. Assets: Machinery ₹1,20,000, Debtors ₹80,000, Stock ₹60,000, Cash ₹75,000. Machinery sold ₹1,05,000; Debtors 10% bad debt, rest realized; Stock realized ₹55,000; Creditors paid ₹66,000; Expenses ₹4,000. V's share of Realisation loss and V's final capital (cash to receive):
options: Loss share ₹10,500; Final capital ₹79,500
Loss share ₹9,000; Final capital ₹81,000
Loss share ₹7,500; Final capital ₹82,500
Loss share ₹13,500; Final capital ₹76,500
questions.explanation: Realisation debit = assets ₹2,60,000 + creditors paid ₹66,000 + expenses ₹4,000 = ₹3,30,000. Realisation credit = creditors ₹70,000 + machinery ₹1,05,000 + debtors ₹72,000 + stock ₹55,000 = ₹3,02,000. Loss = ₹28,000. V’s share in 5:3 ratio = 3/8 x ₹28,000 = ₹10,500. V’s final capital = ₹90,000 - ₹10,500 = ₹79,500.

questions.question: A, B, C dissolve their partnership with PSR 2:2:1 and closing capitals: A = ₹1,20,000, B = ₹80,000, C = ₹50,000 (all after adjusting reserves and accumulated P&L). Total Realisation loss = ₹60,000. C is insolvent and cannot pay his deficiency. Under Garner v Murray, A's total burden (own loss share + Garner's burden) is:
questions.explanation: A’s own share of Realisation loss in the 2:2:1 ratio = ₹60,000 x 2/5 = ₹24,000. C’s capital after bearing his loss is ₹50,000 - ₹12,000 = ₹38,000 credit, so C has no deficiency. Garner v Murray is not triggered. A’s total burden is therefore ₹24,000.

questions.question: On dissolution of firm P-Q-R (PSR 3:2:1), the following are found: (1) Motor vehicle (BV ₹1,00,000) sold to R at ₹90,000; (2) Investment (not in books, discovered) realized ₹15,000; (3) Prepaid insurance (BV ₹4,000) — premium refunded ₹2,500; (4) Unrecorded loan ₹18,000 discovered and paid. Net effect of all 4 on Realisation profit/loss:
options: Loss of ₹14,500
Loss of ₹10,500
Loss of ₹12,500
Profit of ₹5,500
questions.explanation: Motor vehicle loss = ₹1,00,000 - ₹90,000 = ₹10,000. Unrecorded investment realised gives a ₹15,000 gain. Prepaid insurance loss = ₹4,000 - ₹2,500 = ₹1,500. Unrecorded loan paid causes an ₹18,000 loss. Net result = -₹10,000 + ₹15,000 - ₹1,500 - ₹18,000 = ₹14,500 loss.

questions.question: J and K are equal partners. Firm's assets: Machinery ₹80,000, Debtors ₹50,000, Stock ₹40,000, Cash ₹25,000. Liabilities: Creditors ₹60,000, K's Capital ₹70,000 (Cr), J's Capital ₹65,000 (Cr). [Note: Total assets = Total L+C = ₹1,95,000 — balanced]. Dissolution: All assets realized at 90% of BV. Creditors paid 95%. Expenses ₹2,000. Determine the final cash position and each partner's receipt:
questions.explanation: Non-cash assets of ₹1,70,000 realise 90%, or ₹1,53,000. Cash available before creditor payment = ₹25,000 + ₹1,53,000 = ₹1,78,000. Creditors are paid ₹57,000 and expenses are ₹2,000, leaving ₹1,19,000. Realisation loss = ₹16,000, shared equally. J receives ₹65,000 - ₹8,000 = ₹57,000 and K receives ₹70,000 - ₹8,000 = ₹62,000.

questions.question: At dissolution, a firm has a fixed deposit of ₹1,50,000. It matures on the dissolution date, and interest of ₹7,500 is receivable but unrecorded. How should these be treated?
options: FD transferred at ₹1,50,000 to Realisation debit; interest ignored as unrecorded
FD at ₹1,50,000 and unrecorded interest ₹7,500 both transferred to Realisation debit; entire ₹1,57,500 credited when received
FD at ₹1,50,000 transferred to Realisation; interest ₹7,500 credited directly to Partners' Capital A/cs
Both transferred at net ₹1,57,500 by a single entry
questions.explanation: Fixed Deposit (BV ₹1,50,000) is an asset transferred to Realisation debit: Dr. Realisation ₹1,50,000. Unrecorded accrued interest is an unrecorded asset — transferred separately: Dr. Realisation ₹7,500 (OR: Dr. Realisation A/c, Cr. Accrued Interest A/c... but since unrecorded, no accrued interest account exists). When ₹1,57,500 received: Dr. Cash ₹1,57,500, Cr. Realisation ₹1,57,500. Net Realisation impact from FD group: DR (1,50,000 + 7,500) = ₹1,57,500; CR ₹1,57,500 = zero net effect. Interest is effectively a bonus asset fully realized. The profit from interest ₹7,500 flows to partners in PSR via Realisation.

questions.question: X and Y (3:2). After full dissolution: X's Capital A/c shows debit balance of ₹8,000. X is solvent. Y's Capital shows ₹60,000 credit. Cash A/c balance = ₹52,000. What amount does X pay to the firm to close his account?
options: Y receives ₹52,000; X pays ₹8,000 into the firm
X receives ₹8,000; Y receives ₹60,000
X and Y equally split the deficit
X's deficit is absorbed by Y under Garner v Murray
questions.explanation: X's Capital = ₹8,000 (Dr.) — X owes ₹8,000 to the firm. Entry: Dr. Cash A/c ₹8,000, Cr. X's Capital A/c ₹8,000 (clearing X's debit balance). Updated cash = 52,000 + 8,000 = ₹60,000. Y's Capital = ₹60,000 (Cr.) — firm owes Y ₹60,000. Entry: Dr. Y's Capital ₹60,000, Cr. Cash ₹60,000. Cash closes: 60,000 − 60,000 = 0 ✓. X is SOLVENT, so he pays — Garner v Murray doesn't apply. That rule only applies if X were insolvent and unable to pay. Since X can and must pay ₹8,000, the Cash Account is restored to ₹60,000 and Y is fully paid.

questions.question: Which of the following MOST accurately distinguishes the Revaluation Account from the Realisation Account?
options: Revaluation Account only records increase in asset values; Realisation records both increases and decreases
Revaluation Account is prepared when the firm continues to operate (adjusting asset values on reconstitution); Realisation Account is prepared only when the firm is being wound up
Both accounts serve the same purpose but are used at different stages of a firm's life
Realisation Account records only tangible assets; Revaluation records only intangible assets
questions.explanation: This is the critical distinction: Revaluation Account — used at admission of a partner, retirement, death, or change in PSR; the FIRM CONTINUES; assets and liabilities are revalued; the account shows gain or loss on revaluation distributed in OLD PSR; assets are NOT transferred out. Realisation Account — used ONLY at dissolution; firm is being WOUND UP; ALL non-cash assets transferred to debit, all outside liabilities to credit; actual cash proceeds received; balance is profit/loss distributed in PSR; results in complete closure of books. They cannot be substituted for each other.

questions.question: Firm PQR dissolves. Assets include: Debtors ₹40,000 (PBD ₹2,000), Machinery ₹60,000 (Acc. Dep. ₹10,000 shown separately), Stock ₹25,000. All three assets are correctly transferred to Realisation Account. The debit side of Realisation Account from these three assets (considering PBD and Acc. Dep.) is:
questions.explanation: Debtors: Gross BV = ₹40,000; Dr. Realisation ₹40,000. PBD = ₹2,000 (credit balance) goes to CR of Realisation: Dr. PBD ₹2,000, Cr. Realisation ₹2,000. Net debit from debtors = 40,000 − 2,000 = ₹38,000 on DR (net). Machinery: BV = ₹60,000. If Acc. Dep. is a separate account with ₹10,000 Dr. balance... actually Accumulated Depreciation has a CREDIT balance as a contra account. Dr. Realisation ₹60,000; Dr. Acc. Dep. ₹10,000 → this reduces net book value. But in Realisation Account, Machinery goes at GROSS BV or NET BV? At NET BV = ₹50,000. Dr. Realisation ₹50,000; Dr. Acc. Dep. ₹10,000; Cr. Machinery ₹60,000. So Realisation DR from machinery = ₹50,000. Stock: Dr. Realisation ₹25,000. TOTAL DR of Realisation = 38,000 (net debtors) + 50,000 (net machinery) + 25,000 = ₹1,13,000.

questions.question: A and B (3:1) dissolve their firm. After full realisation: Cash available = ₹2,40,000 (after paying all outside creditors and partner loan). B's Capital (adjusted for all items) = ₹50,000 (Cr). All realisation profit has been distributed. Cash to be paid to A is:
questions.explanation: Total cash = ₹2,40,000 (after all outside payments). From this, B receives his capital = ₹50,000. A receives the remaining = 2,40,000 − 50,000 = ₹1,90,000. This must equal A's adjusted Capital balance: Entry Dr. A's Capital ₹1,90,000, Cr. Cash ₹1,90,000; Dr. B's Capital ₹50,000, Cr. Cash ₹50,000. Total cash paid out = ₹2,40,000 = Cash available ✓. Partners receive exactly what is in their capital accounts — no more, no less, in a dissolution without insolvency.

questions.question: At dissolution, if a partner takes over a liability from the firm (e.g., takes over creditors of ₹15,000), the DOUBLE entry for this, given that liability was already transferred to Realisation Account's credit side, is:
options: Dr. Creditors A/c ₹15,000; Cr. Partner's Capital A/c ₹15,000
Dr. Realisation A/c ₹15,000; Cr. Partner's Capital A/c ₹15,000
Dr. Partner's Capital A/c ₹15,000; Cr. Realisation A/c ₹15,000
Dr. Creditors A/c ₹15,000; Cr. Realisation A/c ₹15,000
questions.explanation: Liability was already transferred: Dr. Creditors ₹15,000, Cr. Realisation ₹15,000 (Creditors account = closed). When partner takes over the liability (pays it from personal funds or assumes it): Dr. Realisation A/c ₹15,000 (the firm 'pays' through Realisation), Cr. Partner's Capital A/c ₹15,000 (partner is credited — the firm now owes partner ₹15,000 since partner assumed the liability). Net on Realisation from creditors: CR ₹15,000 (from Step 2) and DR ₹15,000 (from takeover) = zero impact if settled at BV. If partner pays only ₹13,000 (discount ₹2,000): Dr. Realisation ₹13,000; net = CR 15,000 − DR 13,000 = ₹2,000 gain.

questions.question: Partners F and G (3:2). Bank Overdraft ₹30,000 (liability). An unrecorded Building worth ₹50,000 is discovered. F takes both: assumes Bank Overdraft (pays ₹28,000 to bank) and takes Building at agreed value ₹45,000. Bank Overdraft was already on CR of Realisation at ₹30,000. Effect on Realisation Account profit/loss from these two items only:
options: Profit ₹47,000
Profit ₹7,000
Profit ₹2,000
questions.explanation: Bank overdraft of ₹30,000 is settled for ₹28,000, giving a ₹2,000 gain. The unrecorded building has no book value and is taken over by F at ₹45,000, creating a ₹45,000 gain. Total profit from these two items = ₹2,000 + ₹45,000 = ₹47,000.

questions.question: Assertion (A): A partner's loan to the firm does NOT appear in the Realisation Account at dissolution. Reason (R): Under Section 48, partners' loans are repaid after outside creditors but are distinct from partners' capital — they do not form part of the asset-liability realisation process.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
A is true; R is partially correct but incomplete
A is false; R is true
Both A and R are false
questions.explanation: Both A and R are correct, and R fully explains A. Partners' loans do not pass through Realisation Account because: (1) Realisation Account closes OUTSIDE liabilities and OUTSIDE assets — partner loans are internal obligations to specific partners, not third-party claims; (2) Per Section 48, the repayment sequence distinguishes partner loans from outside creditors — they are in a separate category; (3) The settlement: Dr. Partner's Loan A/c, Cr. Cash A/c — a straightforward repayment that bypasses Realisation Account entirely. Any confusion arises from mistaking all liabilities as Realisation items — only OUTSIDE liabilities go through Realisation.

questions.question: R, S, T share profits 4:3:3. At dissolution, after distributing reserves and adjusting losses: R = ₹2,00,000 (Cr), S = ₹1,50,000 (Cr), T = ₹1,00,000 (Cr). Realisation profit = ₹1,00,000. S takes over Machinery at ₹80,000. Cash available after all settlements (excluding partner payments) = ₹4,50,000. S's cash receipt is:
questions.explanation: S receives 3/10 of the ₹1,00,000 Realisation profit = ₹30,000. S’s capital after profit = ₹1,50,000 + ₹30,000 = ₹1,80,000. Since S takes machinery at ₹80,000, S’s cash receipt = ₹1,80,000 - ₹80,000 = ₹1,00,000.

questions.question: At dissolution, the Balance Sheet of ABC firm shows an outstanding salary (to employees) of ₹8,000 and accrued interest on bank loan of ₹3,000 (not yet shown separately from bank loan). Both are outside liabilities. Additionally, an unrecorded claim of ₹5,000 from a supplier is verified and needs to be settled at ₹4,500. How should these three items affect the Realisation Account?
options: Only outstanding salary transferred to Realisation CR; others ignored
All three liabilities: OS Salary (₹8,000 CR), Accrued Interest (₹3,000 CR transferred), and unrecorded claim paid ₹4,500 DR to Realisation
OS Salary and accrued interest go to Partners' Capital; unrecorded claim to Realisation only
All three directly charged to Partners' Capital in PSR without affecting Realisation
